name: Catacombes de Paris
description: The vast, legendary catacombs hold secrets much stranger than stacked bones.

name: Père Lachaise Cemetery
description: France's most famous cemetery, with some of its most curious tombs.

name: Bibliothèque nationale de France (National Library of France)
description: This massive library holds what was once the largest book collection in the world.

name: 59 Rivoli
description: Notorious artist squat renovated into legal studios.

name: Petite Ceinture
description: Abandoned railway line circling the city of Paris.

name: Montmartre Cemetery
description: A horde of stray cats rule in this secluded Parisian cemetery under a bridge.

name: Shakespeare and Company
description: This iconic Parisian bookstore doubles as the "Tumbleweed Hotel" for traveling writers.

name: House of Nicolas Flamel
description: The oldest stone house in Paris was built by its most famous alchemist.

name: Musée des Arts Forains
description: A museum of carnival attractions where you are invited to ride the rides.

name: Rue Crémieux
description: This charming cobbled backstreet is the most Instagramable street in Paris, much to the residents' annoyance.

name: I Love You Wall
description: Paris' linguistic guide to the international language of, yes, love.

name: Museum of the History of Medicine
description: One of the oldest medical collections in Europe focusing on rare surgical instruments.

name: Paris Point Zero
description: This modest marker near Notre Dame identifies the very center of Paris.

name: The Room of Endangered and Extinct Species
description: A haunting collection of the vanished and disappearing natural world.

name: Un Regard Moderne Bookstore
description: A counter-culture bookstore like no other hides in plain sight in the streets of Paris.

name: Jules Lavirotte's 29 Avenue Rapp
description: A scandalous Art Nouveau collaboration that set Paris all atwitter at the turn of the century.

name: La REcyclerie
description: This Parisian café sticks almost exclusively to an ethos of reuse.

name: Gustave Eiffel's Secret Apartment
description: High atop the Eiffel Tower is a small apartment built exclusively to entertain the science elite and make the rest of Paris jealous.

name: Gallery of Paleontology and Comparative Anatomy
description: This museum houses an imposing skeleton army comprised of creatures great and small.

name: Harry's New York Bar
description: A favorite Paris bar for U.S. expats, including Gershwin, Hemingway, and James Bond.

name: Centre Pompidou
description: An "inside out" building in the historical center of Paris, featuring Europe’s largest modern art museum.

name: Foucault's Pendulum
description: 19th century pendulum and a clock restored by a rogue group of guerilla artists.

name: Musee de la Magie
description: A museum of magic with a Sadist past.

name: Musée des Arts et Métiers
description: France's national museum of scientific and industrial instruments.

name: Statue of Liberty at Pont de Grenelle
description: This statue was given to France by the United States to commemorate the centennial of the French Revolution.

name: Salvador Dalí Sundial
description: A surrealist timepiece by the artist of melting clocks.

name: The Gravestone Courtyard
description: Walk upon the mementos of the dead in this Parisian courtyard paved with medieval tombstones.

name: Remnants of the Paris Guillotine
description: Indents in the pavement are all that remain from a grisly death machine in Paris.

name: Musée de Cluny
description: Medieval museum built over Roman baths that features famous tapestries of a lady and a unicorn.

name: Le Louxor Palais du Cinema
description: Egyptian Revival movie palace abandoned for decades and restored in 2013.

name: Musée de la Vie Romantique
description: Down a cobblestone alleyway is a museum dedicated to Romanticism, with a secret garden.

name: Au Bonheur du Jour
description: Behind the pink curtain, Au Bonheur du Jour, an art gallery of erotic archives.

name: Dans le Noir?
description: "In the Dark" Restaurant in Paris offers dinner in complete darkness.

name: WWII Bunker Under Gare de l'Est
description: A subterranean shelter remains intact under a busy Paris railway station.

name: Le Défenseur du Temps (The Defender of Time)
description: A mechanized clock where a man fights off a dragon, crab, and rooster.

name: Sculptures de Décure
description: Exquisite wall sculptures carved in secret by an early quarryman in an obscure corner of the Paris Catacombs.

name: Musée Curie
description: The history of radiation is on safe display at the foot of a radiation science laboratory.

name: Montparnasse Cemetery
description: A Parisian cemetery where the elite and artistic are interred beneath an eccentric collection of tombs.

name: Parc Monceau
description: The ruins of a Duke's bucolic dream, where camels once roamed alongside "Dutch" windmills and faux-Italian vineyards.

name: Montmartre Funicular
description: The water-powered carriages are long gone, but for over a century this funicular railway has been the fastest route to the highest point in Paris.

name: Musée Carnavalet
description: A museum on the history of Paris, including a set of Napoleon's toiletries, Proust's room, and relics of the Revolution.

name: Statue of Death of the University of Medicine
description: Statue of death tucked in the University of Medicine courtyard.

name: Carousel of Extinct and Endangered Animals
description: Known as the "Dodo Manège," this carousel features a herd of animals that have disappeared—or may be gone soon.

name: Danse de la Fontaine Emergente
description: This Parisian fountain is a little bit fantasy and a little bit nightclub.

name: LaBeL RéCuP'
description: An off-the-beaten-path boutique stuffed with an eclectic collection of upcycled goods.

name: Le Chat Noir Collection
description: Original cabaret handbills, posters, and feline artwork are a purrfect highlight of the Museum of Montmartre.

name: The Last Original Standard Metre
description: An overlooked marble is the only surviving in-place 18th century example of the measurement that changed how we define distance.

name: La Promenade Plantée
description: The first green space constructed on an elevated viaduct.

name: Deyrolle Taxidermy
description: Paris' fabled purveyor of exotic taxidermy and natural history curios.

name: Napoleon's Angels
description: Twelve grave-faced marble angels guard the emperor's tomb at Les Invalides.

name: The Heart of Voltaire
description: In the best of all possible worlds, everyone will get a chance to see this statue in which Voltaire's heart is interred.

name: Lion of The Botanical Gardens
description: A majestic lion statue guards this Parisian botanical garden, and feeds on an unsettling item.

name: Au Roi de la Bière
description: Once a 19th-century brasserie, this McDonald's exterior celebrates Alsatian design and the "King of Beer."

name: Roger La Grenouille
description: This frog-themed restaurant was frequented by Picasso, Saint-Exupéry, and more.

name: The Bouquinistes of Paris
description: The tradition of open-air secondhand and antiquarian bookselling in Paris dates back to the Renaissance.

name: The Recumbent Effigy of Victor Noir
description: How did a French journalist’s grave become the sexiest statue in the cemetery?

name: Rungis International Market
description: Walk through spectacular aisles of hanging meat and wagon wheel–sized cheese at the world's largest wholesale food market.

name: Le Musee des Moulages
description: A unrivaled and rather horrifying collection of wax dermatological models.

name: Rue du Chat-qui-Pêche
description: The narrowest street in Paris.

name: Musée des Egouts (Paris Museum of Sewers)
description: The curious underground history of keeping Paris clean.

name: Lasco Project at Palais de Tokyo
description: A modern-day Lascaux cave underneath Paris.

name: Le Chabanais
description: A grotto-like lodge decorated with gilded mosaics, rocks and a noisy waterfall was the setting for the most sumptuous pleasure house of 1900s Europe.

name: Temple de la Sybille (The Temple of Sybille)
description: This structure atop a craggy cliff in one of the most beautiful parks in Paris boasts an unbeatable view.

name: La Conciergerie
description: This former “ante-chamber to the guillotine” has been refurbished to illustrate the brutal history of French justice.

name: The Stravinsky Fountain
description: A series of abstract, silly sculptures represent the works of a great modern classical composer.

name: The Pitié-Salpêtrière Hospital
description: Pitié-Salpêtrière was the dumping grounds for women who received the dreaded diagnosis of "hysterical."

name: Flame of Liberty
description: This life-size replica of the Statue of Liberty's flame is also a tribute to Princess Diana by default.

name: L'Atelier des Lumières (Workshop of Light)
description: An immersive projection art installation uses sound, scale, and light to take visitors inside classic works of art.

name: Julien Aurouze and Co.
description: A Parisian exterminator's shop whose storefront displays stuffed rats hanging from traps.

name: La Chope des Puces: Temple of Gypsy Jazz
description: An iconic and eccentric bar for "Manouche" Jazz music keeps expanding its space—and its influence.

name: 'Les Spécialistes' Fake Door
description: This banal Parisian entranceway is not what it seems.

name: Labrouste Reading Room
description: This off-limits room in the National Library of France is home to a system of pneumatic tubes, once used to bring books to readers.

name: Grande Galerie de l'Évolution
description: Natural history museum celebrating biodiversity with a parade of taxidermy animals.

name: The Duluc Detective Agency
description: Noirish green neon marks the location of one of the oldest private detective agencies in France.

name: Louis XIV's Globes
description: Two awe-inspiring globes from the reign of the Sun King hang from the ceiling of France's national library.

name: Le Passe-Muraille
description: This surreal statue brings a famous French novel about a supernatural cad to arresting life.

name: Musée Edith Piaf
description: This museum in a Paris apartment gives a unique look at the iconic French singer's life.

name: Crypt Beneath St. Joseph des Carmes
description: The recovered bones of martyrs slaughtered during The Terror are on display in this church crypt.

name: Cannonball of the Hotel de Sens
description: A lone cannonball remains lodged in the walls of the Hotel de Sens as a sober reminder of a revolution.

name: Le Comptoir Général
description: This Paris art space celebrates the creativity and ingenuity that springs from abject poverty, especially in Africa.

name: Fountain of Innocents
description: A 16th-century fountain marks the site of a lost medieval cemetery once filled with mass graves.

name: Chapelle Notre Dame de la Medaille Miraculeuse
description: A popular shrine where the Blessed Virgin allegedly appeared in 1830, displaying a holy armchair and two incorruptible remains of Lady Saints.

name: The Etched Mirror Salons of Laperouse Restaurant
description: Where the high court feasted, frolicked, and high courtesans put their royal lovers' diamonds to the test.

name: Arènes de Lutèce
description: Remains of Roman amphitheater hidden in a sleepy quarter of Paris.

name: Musée du quai Branly (Quai Branly Museum)
description: This Parisian gem is one of the most incredible ethnographic museums in the world.

name: Maison de Serge Gainsbourg
description: The former home of France's controversial pop maestro is almost completely covered in ever-changing tributes and graffiti.

name: World's Oldest Basketball Court
description: Despite being considered a thoroughly American sport, basketball's oldest surviving playing field is found in the basement of a Paris YMCA.

name: Cabinet of Joseph Bonnier de La Mosson
description: Hidden away in a modern library are the remains of one of the 18th century's most celebrated wunderkammers.

name: Jardin Alpin (Alpine Garden)
description: This recessed garden protects thousands of mountain plants by creating its own microclimates.

name: Notre Dame du Travail Church
description: The industrial architecture honors the laborers who brought the Exposition Universelle of 1900 to life.

name: Musée National de la Marine
description: Museum on French naval history, with magnificent figureheads, a historic diving suit, and a boat used by Napoleon.

name: Jardin d'Acclimatation
description: In this Parisian garden's grotesque former life as a zoo it fed exotic animals to the bourgeois and put humans on exhibit.

name: Chez Christiane
description: BDSM, 1930s Style.

name: Cours des Miracles (Court of Miracles)
description: Beggars were miraculously "cured" of their fake ailments when they returned home to this 17th century Paris slum each night.

name: Cimetiere de Picpus
description: The headless bodies of more than 1,300 guillotined victims of the Reign of Terror are buried here in mass graves.

name: Villa La Roche by Le Corbusier
description: Architect Le Corbusier built his friend a cubist house  to match his impressive collection of cubist art.

name: Paris Catacombs Golem
description: A mud statue stands in the dark tunnels, seeming to hold up the weight of the city above.

name: Medici Column
description: The spiral staircase hidden inside this giant Parisian column was likely built for Catherine de Medici's astrologer.

name: Arago Medallions
description: 135 bronze medallions mark a French meridian line which once rivaled the hotshot Greenwich timeline.

name: The Relic Crypt of St. Helena at Église Saint-Leu-Saint-Gilles
description: This little-known relic crypt holds stolen parts of a Roman Empress.

name: Passy Cemetery
description: A short distance from the Eiffel Tower is the final resting place of several of the city’s more well-known and interesting characters.

name: Le Stryge
description: This demon atop the Notre-Dame de Paris looks utterly bored.

name: Cimetière du Calvaire
description: The oldest and smallest cemetery in Paris is only open to the public twice a year.

name: Stohrer
description: Order a boozy baba au rhum at the oldest patisserie in Paris.

name: Jean-Paul Marat's Bathtub at Musée Grévin
description: A magical, macabre wax museum, beloved by locals, uses real artifacts to depict an assassination from the French Revolution.

name: Musée de la Chasse et de la Nature (Museum of Hunting and Nature)
description: Dedicated to the art of hunting, with juxtapositions of contemporary art.

name: Musée de la Contrefaçon
description: An odd museum dedicated to French fakes.

name: Debauve & Gallais
description: Marie Antoinette and Napoleon Bonaparte were fans of this 200-year-old French chocolatier.

name: Pont de la Concorde
description: This bridge was constructed from the rubble of the infamous Bastille.

name: Bouillon Julien
description: Marvel at the opulent Art Nouveau interior while dining on dirt-cheap French classics.

name: The Wax and Bone Relics of Saint Vincent de Paul
description: Saintly bones pressed into a wax effigy of a Catholic superstar.

name: Maison de Victor Hugo
description: A museum dedicated to one of Paris’s most famous residents, tucked into a corner of one of the city’s most famous squares.

name: Argonaute Submarine
description: The Argonaute submarine was the French navy's pride and joy in the 1950's.

name: Musée Marmottan Monet
description: Former hunting lodge of a duke who was an avid collector of impressionist works.

name: Grave of Antoine-Augustin Parmentier
description: The tomb of history's greatest potato promoter is surrounded by tubers.

name: Treaty of Paris Plaque
description: A simple plaque on the facade is the only reminder that the treaty ending the American Revolution was signed inside.

name: 145 Rue Lafayette
description: What looks like a normal building is really a hollow facade hiding a subway vent.

name: Doctor Charcot's Library
description: The personal library of the founder of modern neurology is open to visitors.

name: The Grand Lodge of France
description: One of the oldest masonic museums in Paris.

name: The One-Two-Two
description: This innocuous-seeming building is actually the remnant of a major wartime brothel.

name: Beware of Words
description: A French artist's vague warning hangs over Belleville.

name: Musée de la Prefecture de Police
description: A museum which takes visitors to the côté obscure of Paris.

name: Place de la Reunion Fountain
description: Ordinary art transforms to extraordinary thanks to locals' quirky sense of humor.

name: Musée des Plans-Reliefs
description: The Sun King's military models, saved from the Louvre.

name: Chez Aline
description: Grab a life-changing ham sandwich at this former horse butchershop.

name: Execution Site of Jacques de Molay
description: The approximate spot where the last Grand Master of the Knights Templar was put to death.

name: 36 Rue Saint Sulpice
description: An unusual number plate signifies the building's saucy past.

name: Cinémathèque Française
description: This lesser-known Frank Gehry building houses a treasure trove of cinema history.

name: Mundolingua
description: A museum dedicated to linguistics, from how babies learn to talk to endangered and extinct languages.

name: Bouillon Chartier
description: For more than a century, Parisians have come for affordable dishes in a grand converted train station.

name: 'Bouquet of Tulips'
description: One of Jeff Koons’s largest sculptures and his first commemorative work.

name: The Relics of St. Genevieve at St-Etienne-du-Mont
description: The remaining relics of the patroness of Paris lie in a chapel under a striking double spiral staircase.

name: Basilique Notre-Dame-des-Victoires
description: A 400-year-old church covered floor to ceiling in ex-voto offerings from the faithful.

name: Paris Space Invaders
description: The video game-themed street art first popped up in the French city.

name: Georges Méliès's Grave
description: The final resting place of the "father of special effects."

name: Le Musée du Fumeur
description: Dedicated to the history and culture of smoking, this small museum now includes a vape lounge.

name: The Sausage Stained Glass of the Eglise Saint Eustache
description: With a history of slaughter, feast, and famine, remnants of the pork industry decorate this area--even its church.

name: Lines of Cremains at Père Lachaise Cemetery
description: Stripes of human ashes cover a back lawn in the most sought-after burial grounds in Paris.

name: Statue Le Rhinoceros
description: A beautiful Victorian statue shows France's enduring love of the rhinoceros.

name: Notre-Dame-de-Lorette
description: Claude Monet was baptized in this lavishly ornate Paris church.

name: 'Tower'
description: This mural painted by Keith Haring was done without payment as a gift to entertain the children at the Hôpital Necker.

name: Mascarons of Pont Neuf
description: If you look closely at the oldest bridge in Paris, you might notice hundreds of ugly faces leering from its sides.

name: 'Jeune Éléphant pris au Piège' ('Young Elephant Caught in a Trap')
description: This emotive sculpture was originally created for the 1878 World's Fair.

name: El Clan Destino
description: The storefront of a ramshackle Parisian puppet theater.

name: E. Dehillerin
description: Shop at the 200-year-old store where Julia Child stocked up on copper cookware.

name: The Bears of Gobelins
description: During the summer months, giant teddy bears take over Paris near Les Gobelins metro station.

name: Invader Ground Zero
description: A tribute shrine where the Space Invasion street art movement started.

name: Le Droit Humain Masonic Lodge
description: The historic Masonic Lodge in which the first-ever female Mason was initiated.

name: Hotel Lutetia
description: A glamorous hotel that once accommodated French resistance fighters and concentration camp survivors.

name: Gobelins Tapestry Factory
description: This facility has manufactured stunning tapestries for France's royalty ever since the 17th century.

name: Wall of The Drunken Boat
description: A poem by Arthur Rimbaud is inscribed on the wall of a street that, while it is only one block long, plays a key role in multiple novels.

name: Chez Bob de Tunis
description: Fully loaded, Tunisian-style sandwiches are on the menu at this unassuming hole-in-the-wall.

name: Gnomon of Saint-Sulpice
description: This 18th-century sundial was designed to calculate the date of Easter each year.

name: The Wall of Philip II Augustus
description: The remains of the medieval wall that once surrounded Paris with ties to the construction of the Louvre.

name: Monnaie de Paris (Paris Mint)
description: The oldest continuously operating mint in the world that has been making coins since the 9th-century.

name: Witch Rock of Montmartre
description: This oddly shaped boulder in Paris is rumored to be cursed.

name: Au Pied de Cochon
description: Offal and flawless onion soup are on the menu at this restaurant that once catered to the workers of Les Halles.

name: La Bague de Kenza
description: This Algerian sweet shop serves confections in an eye-popping array of hues.

name: Le Tampographe Sardon
description: A letterpress workshop committed to the art of printing, with unique and creative designs.

name: Étienne-Gaspard Robertson's Tomb
description: A phantasmagoric tomb honors a Belgian artist-scientist's contributions to the world of spooky entertainment.

name: Le Moulin de la Galette
description: Montmartre's sole surviving windmill became an iconic landmark for 19th-century painters, artists, and bohemians.

name: Oldest Tree in Paris
description: This 400-year-old tree is actually an immigrant from North America.

name: Napoleon’s Hat at Le Procope
description: The French leader's hat sits in a glass case at the oldest restaurant in Paris.

name: 'Victims of Revolutions'
description: French sculptor Paul Moreau-Vauthier transformed the site of a 19th-century massacre into a haunting memorial.

name: Lavatory de La Madeleine
description: This renovated Art Nouveau bathroom from 1905 is worth the price of admission.

name: Le Timgad
description: Travel back in time at a Moroccan-inspired restaurant that was the height of glamor in the 1970s.

name: 'French Republic or Death' Graffiti
description: For more than a century, revolutionary graffiti has marked the wall of a Parisian church.

name: Maison de la Colombe
description: The site where two doves fell in love.

name: Vaslav Nijinsky’s Grave
description: A melancholy jester sits on the grave of a famous 20th-century ballet dancer and choreographer.

name: Association Française des Amis des Chemins de Fer
description: A model train wonderland hidden beneath Gare de l'Est station.

name: Fourier's Fourth Apple
description: A giant silver apple a day keeps the perils of capitalism away.

name: The Hood
description: Dishes are packed with flavor at one of the only Singaporean restaurants in Paris.

name: Small Cannon of the Palais Royal
description: This small, ingenious cannon once helped regulate Parisian life.

name: Marché Dejean
description: Peruse produce from Senegal, Cameroon, and other West African nations at this sprawling market.

name: Tour Jean-sans-Peur
description: This tower is one of the best-preserved (and least-visited) remnants of medieval Paris.

name: Grave of Philibert Aspairt
description: The tombstone of an unfortunate explorer who disappeared in the Paris Catacombs.

name: Fontaine de la Croix-du-Trahoir
description: Executions were once held near this beautiful 18th-century fountain.

name: Gold 20-stater of Eucratides I
description: The heaviest gold coin ever minted.

name: Les Abattoirs de Vaugirard
description: The site of slaughterhouses that once satisfied Parisians' taste for horse meat is now home to a park and book market.

name: Tang Frères
description: After immigrating to Paris in the 1970s, two brothers from Laos built a grocery store empire.

name: ‘Dénicheur d’Oursons’
description: A gruesome combat between a hunter and a furious momma bear.

name: Gare de Lyon Métro Exotic Garden
description: An underground tropical garden sits inside a Paris Métro Station.

name: Lempicka's Former Studio
description: Former home to Polish artist Tamara de Lempicka's dazzling Art Deco duplex studio.

name: Phono Museum Paris
description: A treasure trove of audio technology, from wax cylinders to juke boxes and record players.

name: Bar Hemingway at the Ritz Paris
description: A cocktail lounge decorated like an American dive bar, in honor of the legendary author Ernest Hemingway.

name: Grande Caryatid
description: This stone angel is one of the biggest caryatids in all of Paris.

name: The Oasis of Aboukir
description: A lush vertical garden blooming in the heart of Paris.

name: Canal Saint-Martin
description: A large portion of this navigable canal runs under Paris's famed boulevards.

name: Site of La Morgue de Paris
description: The location of this small park was once the site of an infamous public morgue.

name: Villa Seurat
description: This charming, flower-filled, paved cul-de-sac is filled with artists' studio-houses built in the 1920s.

name: The Last Public Urinal In Paris
description: The only remaining vespasienne in Paris is a (stinky) relic of Resistance.

name: Museum of Vampires
description: The macabre collection of Jacques Sirgent, an expert scholar on the undead and all of their trappings.

name: Water Tank Beneath Palais Garnier
description: The subterranean "lake" below the Paris opera house inspired the Phantom of the Opera's lair.

name: Paris Padlocks of Love
description: The celebrated eyesore created by starry-eyed sweethearts.

name: Pierre Bazalgues Macabre Antique Stand
description: Skeletons, medical artifacts, and other obscure and precious treasures all displayed in a wooden pharmacist's cabinet.

name: Pep's Maison
description: One of the last umbrella repair shops in Paris upholds the nostalgic art of umbrella making.

name: Museum of Eroticism
description: Inside a former cabaret, seven floors of erotic art.

name: Musée d'Anatomie Delmas-Orfila-Rouvière
description: The largest medical and anatomical collection in France lies hidden from the public.

name: Musée Dupuytren
description: Weird wax anatomical models reside here, as do other anatomical specimens.

name: Alain Baroux's Antique Curiosity Cabinet
description: An antique curios dealer and hybrid taxidermist.

name: Thanksgiving Grocery
description: An all-American grocery store in Paris sells U.S. "cuisine" like Pop-Tarts and Heinz ketchup to homesick ex-pats.

name: Jeannot's Floor
description: The floorboard scrawling of a tragic madman is on public display outside a Paris hospital, much to the dismay of its opponents.

name: Musée de la Poupée
description: Dolls of all shapes and sizes display the eras of femininity in this spooky and beautiful collection.

name: François Richard's Scientific Devices and Odd Machinery
description: A charming flea market shop dedicated to retrofuturistic apparati.

name: The Beverley Adult Cinema
description: A font of vintage porn on the silver screen.

name: Pomze Paris
description: Got apples?

name: Rotary Love Hotel
description: A Belle Epoque bordello turned into a themed love hotel.

name: Francois Daneck's Colonial Concept
description: Collection of antique taxidermy and natural specimens, near one of Paris's oldest flea markets.

name: L'Oisivethé
description: This Parisian café is dedicated to great tea and the art of knitting.

name: Sape & Co
description: A Parisian boutique devoted to la Sape, the elegant Congolese fashion subculture rooted in European colonization.

name: Musée Bricard
description: A curious collection of locks and keys.

name: La Guez Mère
description: Head to this repurposed Renault van for fiery sausages and fries.
